narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 36, you've clearly put in the work to build a competitive profile for law school, including your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school, especially your Year 3 decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.53 average and stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA, which exceeds the average of 3.21, was the most influential positive force, strongly pulling the prediction in favor of you passing the bar. Your LSAT score, close to the dataset average of 36.70, also helped significantly by nudging the odds toward success. Your Year 1 decile of 5 and family income bracket had smaller effects tilting slightly toward higher risk, while your full-time status provided a modest boost since most successful students share that path. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your strengths in GPA and LSAT align well with those who pass, but your lower Year 3 decile ranking set you apart in a way that raised concerns about long-term performance leading to the bar. We understand this rejection feels disappointing, especially given your promising metrics in key areas. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted academic improvement to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through additional undergrad-level work or test prep that mirrors bar demands. You might also explore part-time options or programs with bar passage support tailored to profiles like yours. Keep pushing forward—many students in similar positions have turned things around with focused effort. We're rooting for your success.
